checks.a_analysis: The output contains headings, tables, paragraphs, and lists that correspond directly to the content found in the source PDF. The headings such as 'NC Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) Elevation Data 2024 Data Collection' and '2025 Data Collection Area' are present in the source. The bulleted points on page 3 match the source text exactly. No fabricated body text or invented section headings were identified.
The source PDF does not contain phone numbers, email addresses, or mailing addresses. The output also contains no such information. No contact details were fabricated.

The output represents all three pages of the source PDF. The primary components of each page, including the main titles, tables (legends), descriptive paragraphs on page 1, and the detailed list on page 3, are all included in the structured JSON output. Although the map legend on page 3 is not repeated in the output (it appears in the page 2 section), the information is preserved in the document.

The legend on page 1 ('Phase & Quality') contains 5 entries in the source and is rendered as a table with 5 data rows in the output (element 3). The map legend on page 2 contains 4 categories and is rendered as a table with 4 rows in the output (element 14). The row counts are correct.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: While most text has been captured, there is some omission of text located within the map graphics. However, this missing text (approximately 15-20 words total across the maps) does not exceed 25% of the content for any given section. The main headings, body text, and tables are all present.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The conversion failed to capture specific text data located within the map graphics on pages 1 and 2. On page 1, the map contains two callouts: 'QL 1 data collection completed' and 'Data available February 2025'. On page 2, there is a sub-heading within the map area: 'High Resolution Topographic Refresh Western North Carolina'. These informative strings are absent from the structured text and the specific date 'February 2025' is not included in the image alt text. A government employee relying on the text would miss this availability date and status information.
checks.b_evidence_source: "QL 1 data collection completed" and "Data available February 2025" (Page 1 map callouts); "High Resolution Topographic Refresh Western North Carolina" (Page 2 map title).
checks.b_evidence_output: The text strings are entirely missing from the structured text elements.

checks.a_analysis: The output document faithfully represents the content of the three pages. While certain elements like the '2025 Data Collection Area' title and the Western North Carolina map image appear multiple times, this reflects the intentional repetition in the source document (pages 2 and 3). No instances of unintentional text duplication were found.

The heading hierarchy is preserved. The document uses H1 tags for the main titles of each page. While Page 3's sub-header 'North Carolina Collaboratory' is promoted to H1 (creating a flat hierarchy), this is an acceptable normalization for accessibility. There are no instances of skipped heading levels (e.g., H1 to H3).

The legends present on pages 1 and 2 of the source document have been converted into tables. These tables correctly identify the header row ('Phase & Quality' and 'Western NC Contractor Tile Extents') and the subsequent data rows. The structural organization of these tables accurately represents the content of the legends.

The reading order follows a logical top-to-bottom, left-to-right flow that matches the visual structure of the source PDF. For example, on page 3, the list content which appears next to the map is read before the map image, which is an appropriate order for presenting the textual details followed by the visual representation.
The bulleted list on page 3 of the source document is correctly converted into an unordered list in the output, maintaining the eight distinct points.
No hyperlinks were present in the source document, and none were incorrectly injected into the output.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output maintains the visual organization of the source PDF. The document is structured sequentially according to the original pages. The headers, text blocks, bullet points, and images are placed in a logical order that follows the source's intent. While the HTML uses a single-column flow compared to the PDF's multi-element layout (like text next to maps), this is a standard responsive design transformation and doesn't hinder the user's ability to find and understand the information.

The legends for the maps, which function as tabular data in the source, have been successfully converted into HTML tables. For instance, the 'Phase & Quality' legend from page 1 and the 'Western NC Contractor Tile Extents' legend from pages 2 and 3 are rendered as clear, readable tables with appropriate rows and content. There are no signs of overlapping text or misaligned columns.

All meaningful images from the source PDF are present in the HTML. This includes the statewide North Carolina LiDAR map, the detailed Western North Carolina map, the LiDAR data collection diagram (airplane), and the various organizational logos (DPS, Emergency Management, EMAP Accredited). No broken image icons or missing visual data were identified.

There is no unintended duplication of body content. While certain elements like the Western NC map and organizational logos appear multiple times, this is because they appear on multiple pages in the source document (e.g., the map is on both page 2 and page 3 of the PDF). The HTML faithfully replicates this page-by-page structure.
